article.title: High Temperature Alters Leaf Lipid Membrane Composition Associated with Photochemistry of PSII and Membrane Thermostability in Rice Seedlings

events.event_label: HT reduces spikelet fertility by inhibiting anther dehiscence
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p007.s003: Rice can be cultivated and grows well in a wide geographical and climatic range. PMC9183074.p007.s004: Elevated temperature can impose adverse effects on rice at different growth stages (such as germination, seedling, tillering, reproductive and grain filling stages) and induce reductions in rice yield. PMC9183074.p007.s005: At the flowering stage, HT reduces spikelet fertility by inhibiting anther dehiscence and hence decreasing pollination. PMC9183074.p007.s006: Additionally, at panicle initiation (PI), HT causes spikelet degeneration and damages the development of flower organs. PMC9183074.p007.s007: The reduction in spikelet fertility can lead to a severe reduction in rice yield.

events.event_label: Control condition maintains membrane stability with low electrolyte leakage
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p012.s001: All four rice genotypes under the control condition displayed good membrane stability as indicated by low EL values ranging from 3.24% to 4.16% (Figure 2A). PMC9183074.p012.s002: When subjected to HT stress, cellular membranes were damaged, and all rice genotypes showed significantly higher EL. PMC9183074.p012.s003: The membranes of the heat-sensitive check, IR64, was most seriously damaged, showing a nine-fold increase in EL, while those of N22 showed the highest integrity with only a 7.15% increase in EL. PMC9183074.p012.s004: Of the two Thai cultivars, CN1 maintained greater membrane stability with its EL value similar to that of N22.

events.event_label: HT changes abundance of erucic acid
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p027.s003: Among saturated fatty acids, arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0) (Table 1) sharply increased in N22, CN1 and IR64, while these acids significantly reduced in KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s004: In addition, erucic acid (C22:3), a long-chain unsaturated fatty acid, increased in response to HT in N22 and CN1, but significantly reduced in KDML105 and IR64 (Table 2). PMC9183074.p027.s005: Long chain fatty acids (C20 to C36) are known to be precursors for cuticular wax synthesis. PMC9183074.p027.s006: A significant increase in long-chain fatty acids could be related to enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax and hence thicker cuticles in these cultivars. PMC9183074.p027.s007: Thicker cuticles could help reduce excessive transpirational water loss during HT stress and increase light dispersion; therefore, alleviated cellular damage was evidenced by lower EL and higher Fv/Fm, particularly in N22 and CN1, compared to those of KDML105.

events.event_label: HT impairs PSII function including peripheral antenna complex and electron transport
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p004.s006: HT affects PSII by significantly decreasing its function. PMC9183074.p004.s007: Several aspects of HT effects on PSII have been reported, including the disassociation of the PSII peripheral antenna complex from the core complex, inactivation and disassociation of oxygen-evolving PSII complexes, and inhibition of electron transport from QA to QB. PMC9183074.p004.s008: The photosynthetic carbon fixation is disrupted by temperatures above optimum for plant function. PMC9183074.p004.s009: This commonly coincides with chlorophyll degradation (caused by reactive oxygen species; ROS) resulting in rapid leaf senescence.

events.event_label: HT stress impairs membrane stability, increasing electrolyte leakage and damaging cellular membranes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p012.s001: All four rice genotypes under the control condition displayed good membrane stability as indicated by low EL values ranging from 3.24% to 4.16% (Figure 2A). PMC9183074.p012.s002: When subjected to HT stress, cellular membranes were damaged, and all rice genotypes showed significantly higher EL. PMC9183074.p012.s003: The membranes of the heat-sensitive check, IR64, was most seriously damaged, showing a nine-fold increase in EL, while those of N22 showed the highest integrity with only a 7.15% increase in EL. PMC9183074.p012.s004: Of the two Thai cultivars, CN1 maintained greater membrane stability with its EL value similar to that of N22.

events.event_label: High Temperature affects leaf lipid membrane composition and thermostability

events.event_label: HT alters fatty acid abundance
Long-chain fatty acids enhance photosynthetic performance and cuticular wax synthesis
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s006: N22 and CN1 showed a higher percentage reduction of unsaturated fatty acids (C18:2 and C18:3), which are the major components of the thylakoid membrane, rendering the optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and intactness of PSII complex. PMC9183074.p002.s007: Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions. PMC9183074.p002.s008: Dramatic increases in long-chain fatty acids may lead to cuticular wax synthesis which provides protective roles for heat tolerance. PMC9183074.p002.s009: Thus, the reduction in unsaturated fatty acid composition of the thylakoid membrane and dramatic increases in long-chain fatty acids may lead to high photosynthetic performance and an enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax which further provided additional protective roles for heat tolerance ability in rice.

PMC9183074.p007.s015: Previous reports in rice showed that HT led to significantly increased lipid peroxidation and electrolyte leakage, in cvs. PMC9183074.p007.s016: Dular, IR64 and KDML105 at seedling, and in cvs.

events.event_label: HT decreases total fatty acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p014.s001: Total fatty acids in seedlings of all four rice cultivars after an exposure to HT sharply reduced by 1.4–2.0-fold compared to the controls (Figure 3). PMC9183074.p014.s002: Significant differences in total fatty acids were noted among genotypes. PMC9183074.p014.s003: The highest total fatty acid content was found in CN1 for both the non-stress (10.769 µg µL−1) and HT-stressed (7.608 µg µL−1) plants. PMC9183074.p014.s004: The HT-stressed plants of N22, KDML105 and IR 64 had similar contents of total fatty acids, varying from 3.89 to 7.61 µg µL−1.

events.event_label: MDA and EL associate with membrane thermostability
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p025.s003: HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death. PMC9183074.p025.s004: MDA content and EL are sufficient indicators of membrane thermostability, and they can be considered directly related to the degree of plant damage by HT. PMC9183074.p025.s005: In this study, CN1 and N22 exhibited slightly decreased membrane thermostability, as indicated by a small increase in EL and MDA content (Figure 2), but IR64 and aromatic Thai rice KDML105 displayed a sharp increase in EL and MDA content under HT (Figure 2). PMC9183074.p025.s006: Pansarakham et al. previously reported that MDA and EL of rice cvs. PMC9183074.p025.s007: IR64, KDML105, and PTT1 significantly increased after an exposure to 42 °C for 7 days while those of Dular and N22 remained unchanged.

events.event_label: HT increases MDA and EL
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p025.s003: HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death. PMC9183074.p025.s004: MDA content and EL are sufficient indicators of membrane thermostability, and they can be considered directly related to the degree of plant damage by HT. PMC9183074.p025.s005: In this study, CN1 and N22 exhibited slightly decreased membrane thermostability, as indicated by a small increase in EL and MDA content (Figure 2), but IR64 and aromatic Thai rice KDML105 displayed a sharp increase in EL and MDA content under HT (Figure 2). PMC9183074.p025.s006: Pansarakham et al. previously reported that MDA and EL of rice cvs. PMC9183074.p025.s007: IR64, KDML105, and PTT1 significantly increased after an exposure to 42 °C for 7 days while those of Dular and N22 remained unchanged.

events.event_label: Fatty acids present in thylakoid membrane
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s012: The major types of fatty acids that constitute lipids (MGDG, DGDG, SQDG and PG) in the plant thylakoid membrane are palmitic acid (C16:0), palmitoleic acid (C16:1), roughanic acid (C16:3), oleic acid (C18:1), linoleic acid (C18:2), and linolenic acid (C18:3). PMC9183074.p026.s013: Unsaturated fatty acids constitute 75% of total fatty acids in the thylakoid membrane, while the saturated fatty acid composition was approximately 25%. PMC9183074.p026.s014: The total saturated fatty acid content under HT remained unchanged in CN1 but significantly declined in KDML105 (Figure 4, Table 1). PMC9183074.p026.s015: CN1 also contained the highest, and KDML105 the lowest amount of saturated fatty acids under HT. PMC9183074.p026.s016: This suggested that, in CN1, lipid saturation level was well maintained under HT when associated with lower lipid peroxidation, a smaller increase in membrane fluidity, and lower EL (Figure 2).

events.event_label: fatty acid composition associates with heat tolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p043.s006: Better maintenance of membrane thermostability and thicker cuticles in heat-tolerant genotypes lead to less electrolyte leakage, lower lipid peroxidation and stable Fv/Fm compared to the more sensitive genotypes. PMC9183074.p043.s007: Thus, this study suggested that there may be some relationships between the lipid composition of the thylakoid membrane and cuticle characteristics in a rice leaf, and their ability to tolerate HT. PMC9183074.p043.s008: From this study, changes in leaf fatty acid compositions can potentially be used as indicators for heat tolerance in rice.

events.event_label: Unsaturated fatty acids detected in seedlings
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p019.s001: A total of nine unsaturated fatty acids were identified, and the contents under the control and HT treatment are shown in Table 2. PMC9183074.p019.s002: Six-unsaturated fatty acids including linoleic acid (C18:2), γ-Linolenic acid (C18:3n6), linolenic acid (C18:3), cis-5,8,11,14,17-eicosapentaenoic acid (C20:5), oleic acid (C18:1) and erucic acid (C22:1) were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars under both conditions. PMC9183074.p019.s003: Cis-11,14-eicosadienoic acid (C20:2) which was present in the non-stress seedlings of all cultivars completely disappeared from all HT-stressed seedlings. PMC9183074.p019.s004: In the control seedlings, this acid was found in slightly greater amounts in the Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105.

events.event_label: HT alters fatty acid composition
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p129.s001: Effect of HT on mono- and polyunsaturated fatty acids in four rice cultivars (N22, CN1, KDML105 and IR64). PMC9183074.p129.s002: Each bar represents mean ± SE (n = 3–4). PMC9183074.p129.s003: Asterisk (*) indicates significant difference between control and HT in mono- or poly unsaturated fatty acid and in each rice cultivar at p ≤ 0.05 by t test. PMC9183074.p129.s004: Different capital letters indicate significant difference of control group in different rice cultivars at p ≤ 0.05 by ANOVA and DMRT, and small letters indicate significant difference of HT in different rice cultivars at p ≤ 0.05 by ANOVA and DMRT.

events.event_label: Lipid classes localized in thylakoid membrane
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s006: A comparison between the two Thai rice cultivars revealed that under HT, CN1 showed the lowest reduction compared to the control and contained the highest total fatty acid content, but KDML105 showed the highest reduction and the lowest amount of total fatty acids (Figure 3, Table 1). PMC9183074.p026.s007: This suggested that HT significantly changed total leaf fatty acids, and different genotypes were affected to different degrees. PMC9183074.p026.s008: The thylakoid membrane is reported to be the most sensitive site severely damaged by heat stress and the degree of damage is related to plant survival. PMC9183074.p026.s009: The major types of lipids in the thylakoid membrane are galactolipids (monogalactosyldiacylglycerol, MGDG and digalactosyldiacylglycerol, DGDG), sulfolipids (sulfoquinovosyldiacylglycerol, SQDG), and phospholipids (phosphatidylglycerol, PG). PMC9183074.p026.s010: Of all lipids in thylakoids, MGDG and DGDG can reach 52% and 26%, respectively.

events.event_label: Unsaturated fatty acids and ROS are substrates for membrane lipid peroxidation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p005.s001: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive site affected by environmental stimulation, including HT. PMC9183074.p005.s002: HT has been reported to be associated with disrupting cell membranes, breaking down cell compartmentalization, leading to solute leakage and affecting cellular functions that cause physiological injuries and leaf senescence. PMC9183074.p005.s003: The disruption of a cellular membrane by HT is related to the increase in membrane lipid peroxidation, which occurs when double bonds of unsaturated fatty acids are attacked by ROS.

PMC9183074.p126.s004: Different capital letters indicate significant difference of control group in different rice cultivars at p ≤ 0.05 by ANOVA and DMRT, and small letters indicate significant difference of HT in different rice cultivars at p ≤ 0.05 by ANOVA and DMRT.

events.event_label: HT disrupts PSII and photosynthetic processes leading to leaf senescence
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p004.s006: HT affects PSII by significantly decreasing its function. PMC9183074.p004.s007: Several aspects of HT effects on PSII have been reported, including the disassociation of the PSII peripheral antenna complex from the core complex, inactivation and disassociation of oxygen-evolving PSII complexes, and inhibition of electron transport from QA to QB. PMC9183074.p004.s008: The photosynthetic carbon fixation is disrupted by temperatures above optimum for plant function. PMC9183074.p004.s009: This commonly coincides with chlorophyll degradation (caused by reactive oxygen species; ROS) resulting in rapid leaf senescence.

events.event_label: HT reduces electrolyte leakage increase and prevents MDA accumulation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s003: Twenty-eight-day-old seedlings of two Thai rice cultivars (CN1 and KDML105), a standard heat tolerance (N22), and a heat sensitive (IR64) rice genotype were treated at 42 °C for 7 days. PMC9183074.p002.s004: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm). PMC9183074.p002.s005: Compared to KDML105 and IR64, CN1 was more tolerant of HT, showing a lower increase in EL and MDA, and less reduction in Fv/Fm. PMC9183074.p002.s006: N22 and CN1 showed a higher percentage reduction of unsaturated fatty acids (C18:2 and C18:3), which are the major components of the thylakoid membrane, rendering the optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and intactness of PSII complex. PMC9183074.p002.s007: Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions.

events.event_label: HT induces membrane lipid peroxidation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p005.s001: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive site affected by environmental stimulation, including HT. PMC9183074.p005.s002: HT has been reported to be associated with disrupting cell membranes, breaking down cell compartmentalization, leading to solute leakage and affecting cellular functions that cause physiological injuries and leaf senescence. PMC9183074.p005.s003: The disruption of a cellular membrane by HT is related to the increase in membrane lipid peroxidation, which occurs when double bonds of unsaturated fatty acids are attacked by ROS.

events.event_label: Long-chain fatty acids promote cuticular wax synthesis contributing to heat tolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s006: N22 and CN1 showed a higher percentage reduction of unsaturated fatty acids (C18:2 and C18:3), which are the major components of the thylakoid membrane, rendering the optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and intactness of PSII complex. PMC9183074.p002.s007: Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions. PMC9183074.p002.s008: Dramatic increases in long-chain fatty acids may lead to cuticular wax synthesis which provides protective roles for heat tolerance. PMC9183074.p002.s009: Thus, the reduction in unsaturated fatty acid composition of the thylakoid membrane and dramatic increases in long-chain fatty acids may lead to high photosynthetic performance and an enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax which further provided additional protective roles for heat tolerance ability in rice.

events.event_label: long-chain fatty acids enhance cuticular wax synthesis and photosynthetic performance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s006: N22 and CN1 showed a higher percentage reduction of unsaturated fatty acids (C18:2 and C18:3), which are the major components of the thylakoid membrane, rendering the optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and intactness of PSII complex. PMC9183074.p002.s007: Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions. events.event_label: HT changes fatty acid saturation influencing membrane fluidity
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p006.s003: Thus, to decrease membrane fluidity under HT, lipid unsaturations could be decreased by downregulating the fatty acid desaturase (FAD) gene. PMC9183074.p006.s004: Maintaining the optimum composition of unsaturated fatty acids is important in determining cellular membrane fluidity under HT. PMC9183074.p006.s005: For heat-tolerant plants under HT, the degree of saturated fatty acids increases in both thylakoid membranes and cellular membranes. PMC9183074.p006.s006: This enhances lipid heat stability resulting in thermal tolerance in diverse species. PMC9183074.p006.s007: Some studies showed that changed lipid saturation was involved in the plant’s ability to tolerate heat.

events.event_label: HT exposure alters monounsaturated fatty acid levels
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p020.s001: Changes in mono- and polyunsaturated fatty acids are shown in Figure 5. PMC9183074.p020.s002: Monounsaturated fatty acids in all rice cultivars were several folds lower than polyunsaturated fatty acids. PMC9183074.p020.s003: Upon HT exposure, monounsaturated fatty acids significantly increased in N22 and IR64, but significantly decreased in Thai rice, CN1. PMC9183074.p020.s004: Polyunsaturated fatty acids in seedlings of all rice cultivars markedly decreased under HT.

events.event_label: HT impairs thylakoid membrane and PSII photochemistry
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p025.s009: Thylakoid membrane is the site where light harvesting pigments, PSII, PSI and electron carrier proteins are embedded. PMC9183074.p025.s010: Thus, the thylakoid membrane-increased fluidity due to HT resulted in weakness in the bonding between lipid and electron carrier protein molecules. PMC9183074.p025.s011: Consequently, the light reaction taking place in the thylakoid membrane was inhibited, resulting in a reduction in the efficiency of the PSII photochemistry as seen in IR64 and KDML105 (Figure 1).

events.event_label: HT decreases total fatty acid content
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s003: Diverse plant lipids consist of polyunsaturated fatty acids, mainly linoleic acid (C18:2) and linolenic acid (C18:3), monounsaturated fatty acids, mainly oleic acid (C18:1) and saturated fatty acids, mainly palmitic acid (C16:0) and stearic acid (C18:0). PMC9183074.p026.s004: An insight into the relationship between the observed physiological parameters under HT and alterations in leaf fatty acid content and composition was manifested in this study. PMC9183074.p026.s005: Under HT, total fatty acid content decreased in all rice cultivars (Figure 3, Table 1). PMC9183074.p026.s006: A comparison between the two Thai rice cultivars revealed that under HT, CN1 showed the lowest reduction compared to the control and contained the highest total fatty acid content, but KDML105 showed the highest reduction and the lowest amount of total fatty acids (Figure 3, Table 1). PMC9183074.p026.s007: This suggested that HT significantly changed total leaf fatty acids, and different genotypes were affected to different degrees.

events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s003: Twenty-eight-day-old seedlings of two Thai rice cultivars (CN1 and KDML105), a standard heat tolerance (N22), and a heat sensitive (IR64) rice genotype were treated at 42 °C for 7 days. PMC9183074.p002.s004: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm). PMC9183074.p002.s005: Compared to KDML105 and IR64, CN1 was more tolerant of HT, showing a lower increase in EL and MDA, and less reduction in Fv/Fm. PMC9183074.p002.s007: Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions.

events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p007.s003: Rice can be cultivated and grows well in a wide geographical and climatic range. events.event_label: saturated fatty acids detected in rice seedlings
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p017.s001: A total of nine saturated fatty acids were identified from rice seedlings, and the content of each acid under the control and HT treatment are displayed in Table 1. PMC9183074.p017.s002: Eight saturated fatty acids, namely lauric acid (C12:0), myristic acid (C14:0), pentadecanoic acid (C15:0), palmitic acid (C16:0), stearic acid (C18:0), arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0), were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars in both control and HT conditions. PMC9183074.p017.s003: Only one saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), was found only in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p017.s004: Moreover, capric acid contents in HT-stressed CN1 and KDML105 exhibited significant increments.

events.event_label: HT alters saturated fatty acid levels
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p017.s006: Palmitic acid and stearic acid markedly decreased with HT in all cultivars. PMC9183074.p017.s007: Three saturated fatty acids including arachidic acid, behenic acid, and lignoceric acid in N22, CN1 and IR64 were significantly increased after HT treatment, but in KDML105, the contents of these acids declined significantly.

events.event_label: HT induces membrane fluidity increase, lipid peroxidation and MDA accumulation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p025.s001: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT. PMC9183074.p025.s002: The thylakoid membrane, where the photosynthetic light reaction occurs, is also highly thermal sensitive. PMC9183074.p025.s003: HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death. events.event_label: Unsaturated fatty acids support membrane fluidity and heat tolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p006.s012: Moreover, soybean and peanut also responded to HT by decreasing lipid unsaturation levels to maintain membrane fluidity, and the magnitudes of reduction were greater for heat-tolerant than for heat-sensitive genotypes. PMC9183074.p006.s013: In contrast, some experiments demonstrated that higher degrees of unsaturated fatty acids can maintain membrane fluidity under HT. PMC9183074.p006.s014: For example, tall fescue genotypes that exhibited an increased degree of unsaturated fatty acids showed an improvement in heat tolerance.

events.event_label: HT affects saturated fatty acids in thylakoid membrane
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s012: The major types of fatty acids that constitute lipids (MGDG, DGDG, SQDG and PG) in the plant thylakoid membrane are palmitic acid (C16:0), palmitoleic acid (C16:1), roughanic acid (C16:3), oleic acid (C18:1), linoleic acid (C18:2), and linolenic acid (C18:3). PMC9183074.p026.s013: Unsaturated fatty acids constitute 75% of total fatty acids in the thylakoid membrane, while the saturated fatty acid composition was approximately 25%. PMC9183074.p026.s014: The total saturated fatty acid content under HT remained unchanged in CN1 but significantly declined in KDML105 (Figure 4, Table 1). PMC9183074.p026.s015: CN1 also contained the highest, and KDML105 the lowest amount of saturated fatty acids under HT. PMC9183074.p026.s016: This suggested that, in CN1, lipid saturation level was well maintained under HT when associated with lower lipid peroxidation, a smaller increase in membrane fluidity, and lower EL (Figure 2).

events.event_label: HT increases erucic acid, a precursor for cuticular wax synthesis
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p027.s003: Among saturated fatty acids, arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0) (Table 1) sharply increased in N22, CN1 and IR64, while these acids significantly reduced in KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s004: In addition, erucic acid (C22:3), a long-chain unsaturated fatty acid, increased in response to HT in N22 and CN1, but significantly reduced in KDML105 and IR64 (Table 2). PMC9183074.p027.s005: Long chain fatty acids (C20 to C36) are known to be precursors for cuticular wax synthesis. PMC9183074.p027.s006: A significant increase in long-chain fatty acids could be related to enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax and hence thicker cuticles in these cultivars. PMC9183074.p027.s007: Thicker cuticles could help reduce excessive transpirational water loss during HT stress and increase light dispersion; therefore, alleviated cellular damage was evidenced by lower EL and higher Fv/Fm, particularly in N22 and CN1, compared to those of KDML105.

events.event_label: HT increases MDA and EL, associated with reduced membrane thermostability
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p025.s003: HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death. PMC9183074.p025.s004: MDA content and EL are sufficient indicators of membrane thermostability, and they can be considered directly related to the degree of plant damage by HT. PMC9183074.p025.s005: In this study, CN1 and N22 exhibited slightly decreased membrane thermostability, as indicated by a small increase in EL and MDA content (Figure 2), but IR64 and aromatic Thai rice KDML105 displayed a sharp increase in EL and MDA content under HT (Figure 2). PMC9183074.p025.s006: Pansarakham et al. previously reported that MDA and EL of rice cvs. PMC9183074.p025.s007: IR64, KDML105, and PTT1 significantly increased after an exposure to 42 °C for 7 days while those of Dular and N22 remained unchanged.

events.event_label: HT alters fatty acid composition and thylakoid membrane, reducing unsaturated fatty acids and enhancing membrane stability
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p043.s001: HT caused differential alterations in fatty acid profiles, especially those comprising the thylakoid membrane in rice cultivars differing in heat tolerance. PMC9183074.p043.s002: In the case of N22 and CN1, their leaves slightly decreased the degree of lipid thylakoid membrane composition in the amount of palmitic acid (C16:0), linolenic acid (C18:2), γ-linolenic acid (C18:3n6) and linolenic acid (C18:3) and increased the amount of palmitoleic acid (C16:1). PMC9183074.p043.s003: The greater reduction in unsaturated fatty acids under HT, especially in N22, was related to maintenance of optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and increased membrane stability. PMC9183074.p043.s004: Moreover, in N22 and CN1, long-chain fatty acids, which serve as precursors of cuticular wax synthesis, namely arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0), lignoceric acid (24:0) and erucic acid (C22:1), also increased.

events.event_label: HT decreases photosynthetic efficiency and increases ROS
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p024.s001: High temperature (HT) is harmful to plant growth and survival. PMC9183074.p024.s002: It decreases photosynthetic efficiency and yield. PMC9183074.p024.s003: HT can induce excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, resulting in cellular membrane injury, impaired photosynthetic mechanism and oxygen evolving complex associated with PSII, and inhibition of protein synthesis. PMC9183074.p024.s004: Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction.

events.event_label: HT impairs PSII
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p004.s006: HT affects PSII by significantly decreasing its function. PMC9183074.p004.s007: Several aspects of HT effects on PSII have been reported, including the disassociation of the PSII peripheral antenna complex from the core complex, inactivation and disassociation of oxygen-evolving PSII complexes, and inhibition of electron transport from QA to QB. PMC9183074.p004.s008: The photosynthetic carbon fixation is disrupted by temperatures above optimum for plant function. PMC9183074.p004.s009: This commonly coincides with chlorophyll degradation (caused by reactive oxygen species; ROS) resulting in rapid leaf senescence.

events.event_label: HT damages cellular membranes leading to leaf senescence
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p005.s001: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive site affected by environmental stimulation, including HT. PMC9183074.p005.s002: HT has been reported to be associated with disrupting cell membranes, breaking down cell compartmentalization, leading to solute leakage and affecting cellular functions that cause physiological injuries and leaf senescence. PMC9183074.p005.s003: The disruption of a cellular membrane by HT is related to the increase in membrane lipid peroxidation, which occurs when double bonds of unsaturated fatty acids are attacked by ROS.

PMC9183074.p007.s015: Previous reports in rice showed that HT led to significantly increased lipid peroxidation and electrolyte leakage, in cvs. PMC9183074.p007.s016: Dular, IR64 and KDML105 at seedling, and in cvs.

events.event_label: saturated fatty acids detected in rice seedlings
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p017.s001: A total of nine saturated fatty acids were identified from rice seedlings, and the content of each acid under the control and HT treatment are displayed in Table 1. PMC9183074.p017.s002: Eight saturated fatty acids, namely lauric acid (C12:0), myristic acid (C14:0), pentadecanoic acid (C15:0), palmitic acid (C16:0), stearic acid (C18:0), arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0), were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars in both control and HT conditions. PMC9183074.p017.s003: Only one saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), was found only in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p017.s004: Moreover, capric acid contents in HT-stressed CN1 and KDML105 exhibited significant increments.

events.event_label: HT decreases fatty acid abundance
HT decreases leaf fatty acid content
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s003: Diverse plant lipids consist of polyunsaturated fatty acids, mainly linoleic acid (C18:2) and linolenic acid (C18:3), monounsaturated fatty acids, mainly oleic acid (C18:1) and saturated fatty acids, mainly palmitic acid (C16:0) and stearic acid (C18:0). PMC9183074.p026.s004: An insight into the relationship between the observed physiological parameters under HT and alterations in leaf fatty acid content and composition was manifested in this study. PMC9183074.p026.s005: Under HT, total fatty acid content decreased in all rice cultivars (Figure 3, Table 1). PMC9183074.p026.s006: A comparison between the two Thai rice cultivars revealed that under HT, CN1 showed the lowest reduction compared to the control and contained the highest total fatty acid content, but KDML105 showed the highest reduction and the lowest amount of total fatty acids (Figure 3, Table 1). PMC9183074.p026.s007: This suggested that HT significantly changed total leaf fatty acids, and different genotypes were affected to different degrees. PMC9183074.p026.s008: The thylakoid membrane is reported to be the most sensitive site severely damaged by heat stress and the degree of damage is related to plant survival. PMC9183074.p026.s009: The major types of lipids in the thylakoid membrane are galactolipids (monogalactosyldiacylglycerol, MGDG and digalactosyldiacylglycerol, DGDG), sulfolipids (sulfoquinovosyldiacylglycerol, SQDG), and phospholipids (phosphatidylglycerol, PG). PMC9183074.p026.s010: Of all lipids in thylakoids, MGDG and DGDG can reach 52% and 26%, respectively.

events.event_label: unsaturated fatty acids affect membrane fluidity
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p006.s003: Thus, to decrease membrane fluidity under HT, lipid unsaturations could be decreased by downregulating the fatty acid desaturase (FAD) gene. PMC9183074.p006.s004: Maintaining the optimum composition of unsaturated fatty acids is important in determining cellular membrane fluidity under HT. PMC9183074.p006.s005: For heat-tolerant plants under HT, the degree of saturated fatty acids increases in both thylakoid membranes and cellular membranes. PMC9183074.p006.s006: This enhances lipid heat stability resulting in thermal tolerance in diverse species. PMC9183074.p006.s007: Some studies showed that changed lipid saturation was involved in the plant’s ability to tolerate heat. PMC9183074.p006.s012: Moreover, soybean and peanut also responded to HT by decreasing lipid unsaturation levels to maintain membrane fluidity, and the magnitudes of reduction were greater for heat-tolerant than for heat-sensitive genotypes. PMC9183074.p006.s013: In contrast, some experiments demonstrated that higher degrees of unsaturated fatty acids can maintain membrane fluidity under HT. PMC9183074.p006.s014: For example, tall fescue genotypes that exhibited an increased degree of unsaturated fatty acids showed an improvement in heat tolerance.

PMC9183074.p026.s007: This suggested that HT significantly changed total leaf fatty acids, and different genotypes were affected to different degrees. events.event_label: long-chain fatty acids promote cuticular wax synthesis
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s006: N22 and CN1 showed a higher percentage reduction of unsaturated fatty acids (C18:2 and C18:3), which are the major components of the thylakoid membrane, rendering the optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and intactness of PSII complex. PMC9183074.p002.s007: Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions. PMC9183074.p002.s008: Dramatic increases in long-chain fatty acids may lead to cuticular wax synthesis which provides protective roles for heat tolerance. PMC9183074.p002.s009: Thus, the reduction in unsaturated fatty acid composition of the thylakoid membrane and dramatic increases in long-chain fatty acids may lead to high photosynthetic performance and an enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax which further provided additional protective roles for heat tolerance ability in rice.

events.event_label: HT decreases total fatty acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p014.s001: Total fatty acids in seedlings of all four rice cultivars after an exposure to HT sharply reduced by 1.4–2.0-fold compared to the controls (Figure 3). PMC9183074.p014.s002: Significant differences in total fatty acids were noted among genotypes. PMC9183074.p014.s003: The highest total fatty acid content was found in CN1 for both the non-stress (10.769 µg µL−1) and HT-stressed (7.608 µg µL−1) plants. PMC9183074.p014.s004: The HT-stressed plants of N22, KDML105 and IR 64 had similar contents of total fatty acids, varying from 3.89 to 7.61 µg µL−1. PMC9183074.p014.s005: The highest percentage reduction in total fatty acids after HT exposure was found in KDML105 at 50.94% and the lowest in CN1 (29.35%).

events.event_label: HT reduces Fv/Fm
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p024.s009: Dular is known to be a heat-tolerant rice variety comparable to N22. PMC9183074.p024.s010: However, Sailaja et al. stated that in response to heat stress, Fv/Fm in N22 was stable at the reproductive stage but reduced at the vegetative stage. PMC9183074.p024.s011: This indicated that the ability to maintain PSII efficiency varies with developmental stage. PMC9183074.p024.s012: However, Thai rice cv. PMC9183074.p024.s013: CN1 showed the highest Fv/Fm level, although its Fv/Fm declined after HT exposure (Figure 1).

events.event_label: HT damages cellular membranes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p012.s001: All four rice genotypes under the control condition displayed good membrane stability as indicated by low EL values ranging from 3.24% to 4.16% (Figure 2A). PMC9183074.p012.s002: When subjected to HT stress, cellular membranes were damaged, and all rice genotypes showed significantly higher EL. PMC9183074.p012.s003: The membranes of the heat-sensitive check, IR64, was most seriously damaged, showing a nine-fold increase in EL, while those of N22 showed the highest integrity with only a 7.15% increase in EL. PMC9183074.p012.s004: Of the two Thai cultivars, CN1 maintained greater membrane stability with its EL value similar to that of N22.

PMC9183074.p027.s008: Although IR64 showed significant increases in the saturated long-chain fatty acids, its proposed enhanced cuticular wax synthesis might be offset by its weaker antioxidant systems as evidenced by high MDA content, leading to higher ROS-induced cellular damage. PMC9183074.p027.s009: Moreover, saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), under HT and control was found in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s010: In particular, KDML105 showed the highest capric acid content, and it increased significantly after exposure to HT (Figure 5, Table 2). PMC9183074.p027.s012: Capric acid (C10:0) occurs in coconut oil, and it is used as an aromatic chemical in the manufacture of perfumes. PMC9183074.p027.s013: This suggested that Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105, especially KDML105 or Thai Jasmine rice, is an aromatic rice and has volatile aromatic compounds that make the aroma and flavour in the rice. PMC9183074.p027.s014: Thus, capric acid (C10:0) in Thai rice may involve aroma and flavour, not only 2AP.

PMC9183074.p027.s004: In addition, erucic acid (C22:3), a long-chain unsaturated fatty acid, increased in response to HT in N22 and CN1, but significantly reduced in KDML105 and IR64 (Table 2). PMC9183074.p027.s005: Long chain fatty acids (C20 to C36) are known to be precursors for cuticular wax synthesis. PMC9183074.p027.s006: A significant increase in long-chain fatty acids could be related to enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax and hence thicker cuticles in these cultivars. PMC9183074.p027.s007: Thicker cuticles could help reduce excessive transpirational water loss during HT stress and increase light dispersion; therefore, alleviated cellular damage was evidenced by lower EL and higher Fv/Fm, particularly in N22 and CN1, compared to those of KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s009: Moreover, saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), under HT and control was found in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s010: In particular, KDML105 showed the highest capric acid content, and it increased significantly after exposure to HT (Figure 5, Table 2). PMC9183074.p027.s011: Capric acid (C10:0) was not found in N22 and IR64. PMC9183074.p027.s012: Capric acid (C10:0) occurs in coconut oil, and it is used as an aromatic chemical in the manufacture of perfumes. PMC9183074.p027.s013: This suggested that Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105, especially KDML105 or Thai Jasmine rice, is an aromatic rice and has volatile aromatic compounds that make the aroma and flavour in the rice.

events.event_label: HT treatment increases saturated fatty acids (behenic and lignoceric)
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p017.s006: Palmitic acid and stearic acid markedly decreased with HT in all cultivars. PMC9183074.p017.s007: Three saturated fatty acids including arachidic acid, behenic acid, and lignoceric acid in N22, CN1 and IR64 were significantly increased after HT treatment, but in KDML105, the contents of these acids declined significantly.

events.event_label: HT induces lipid peroxidation leading to MDA production
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p025.s001: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT. PMC9183074.p025.s002: The thylakoid membrane, where the photosynthetic light reaction occurs, is also highly thermal sensitive. PMC9183074.p025.s003: HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death. PMC9183074.p025.s004: MDA content and EL are sufficient indicators of membrane thermostability, and they can be considered directly related to the degree of plant damage by HT. PMC9183074.p025.s005: In this study, CN1 and N22 exhibited slightly decreased membrane thermostability, as indicated by a small increase in EL and MDA content (Figure 2), but IR64 and aromatic Thai rice KDML105 displayed a sharp increase in EL and MDA content under HT (Figure 2). PMC9183074.p025.s006: Pansarakham et al. previously reported that MDA and EL of rice cvs. PMC9183074.p025.s007: IR64, KDML105, and PTT1 significantly increased after an exposure to 42 °C for 7 days while those of Dular and N22 remained unchanged.

events.event_label: Unsaturated fatty acids are present in the thylakoid membrane
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s006: A comparison between the two Thai rice cultivars revealed that under HT, CN1 showed the lowest reduction compared to the control and contained the highest total fatty acid content, but KDML105 showed the highest reduction and the lowest amount of total fatty acids (Figure 3, Table 1). PMC9183074.p026.s007: This suggested that HT significantly changed total leaf fatty acids, and different genotypes were affected to different degrees. PMC9183074.p026.s008: The thylakoid membrane is reported to be the most sensitive site severely damaged by heat stress and the degree of damage is related to plant survival. PMC9183074.p026.s009: The major types of lipids in the thylakoid membrane are galactolipids (monogalactosyldiacylglycerol, MGDG and digalactosyldiacylglycerol, DGDG), sulfolipids (sulfoquinovosyldiacylglycerol, SQDG), and phospholipids (phosphatidylglycerol, PG). PMC9183074.p026.s010: Of all lipids in thylakoids, MGDG and DGDG can reach 52% and 26%, respectively. PMC9183074.p026.s012: The major types of fatty acids that constitute lipids (MGDG, DGDG, SQDG and PG) in the plant thylakoid membrane are palmitic acid (C16:0), palmitoleic acid (C16:1), roughanic acid (C16:3), oleic acid (C18:1), linoleic acid (C18:2), and linolenic acid (C18:3). PMC9183074.p026.s013: Unsaturated fatty acids constitute 75% of total fatty acids in the thylakoid membrane, while the saturated fatty acid composition was approximately 25%. PMC9183074.p026.s014: The total saturated fatty acid content under HT remained unchanged in CN1 but significantly declined in KDML105 (Figure 4, Table 1). PMC9183074.p026.s015: CN1 also contained the highest, and KDML105 the lowest amount of saturated fatty acids under HT. PMC9183074.p026.s016: This suggested that, in CN1, lipid saturation level was well maintained under HT when associated with lower lipid peroxidation, a smaller increase in membrane fluidity, and lower EL (Figure 2).

events.event_label: HT decreases total fatty acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p014.s001: Total fatty acids in seedlings of all four rice cultivars after an exposure to HT sharply reduced by 1.4–2.0-fold compared to the controls (Figure 3). PMC9183074.p014.s002: Significant differences in total fatty acids were noted among genotypes. PMC9183074.p014.s003: The highest total fatty acid content was found in CN1 for both the non-stress (10.769 µg µL−1) and HT-stressed (7.608 µg µL−1) plants. PMC9183074.p014.s004: The HT-stressed plants of N22, KDML105 and IR 64 had similar contents of total fatty acids, varying from 3.89 to 7.61 µg µL−1. PMC9183074.p014.s005: The highest percentage reduction in total fatty acids after HT exposure was found in KDML105 at 50.94% and the lowest in CN1 (29.35%).

events.event_label: HT impairs photosynthetic processes (PSII and carbon fixation)
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p004.s006: HT affects PSII by significantly decreasing its function. PMC9183074.p004.s007: Several aspects of HT effects on PSII have been reported, including the disassociation of the PSII peripheral antenna complex from the core complex, inactivation and disassociation of oxygen-evolving PSII complexes, and inhibition of electron transport from QA to QB. PMC9183074.p004.s008: The photosynthetic carbon fixation is disrupted by temperatures above optimum for plant function. PMC9183074.p004.s009: This commonly coincides with chlorophyll degradation (caused by reactive oxygen species; ROS) resulting in rapid leaf senescence.

events.event_label: ROS affects cellular membrane and impairs photosynthetic mechanism
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p024.s001: High temperature (HT) is harmful to plant growth and survival. PMC9183074.p024.s002: It decreases photosynthetic efficiency and yield. PMC9183074.p024.s003: HT can induce excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, resulting in cellular membrane injury, impaired photosynthetic mechanism and oxygen evolving complex associated with PSII, and inhibition of protein synthesis. PMC9183074.p024.s004: Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction.

events.event_label: leaf fatty acid associates_with HT tolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s001: Rice cultivated in the tropics is exposed to high temperature (HT) stress which threatens its growth and survival. PMC9183074.p002.s002: This study aimed at characterizing the HT response in terms of PSII efficiency and membrane stability, and to identify leaf fatty acid changes that may be associated with HT tolerance or sensitivity of rice genotypes. PMC9183074.p002.s003: Twenty-eight-day-old seedlings of two Thai rice cultivars (CN1 and KDML105), a standard heat tolerance (N22), and a heat sensitive (IR64) rice genotype were treated at 42 °C for 7 days. PMC9183074.p002.s004: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm).

events.event_label: unsaturated fatty acids is_detected_in thylakoid membrane
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s003: Twenty-eight-day-old seedlings of two Thai rice cultivars (CN1 and KDML105), a standard heat tolerance (N22), and a heat sensitive (IR64) rice genotype were treated at 42 °C for 7 days. PMC9183074.p002.s004: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm). PMC9183074.p002.s005: Compared to KDML105 and IR64, CN1 was more tolerant of HT, showing a lower increase in EL and MDA, and less reduction in Fv/Fm. PMC9183074.p002.s006: N22 and CN1 showed a higher percentage reduction of unsaturated fatty acids (C18:2 and C18:3), which are the major components of the thylakoid membrane, rendering the optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and intactness of PSII complex. PMC9183074.p002.s007: Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions.

events.event_label: HT increases_abundance_of membrane fluidity
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p006.s001: The fluidity of lipid membrane is important for membrane function that depends on the interaction between lipid and protein compositions. PMC9183074.p006.s002: The increase in membrane fluidity resulted from an HT-induced higher degree of lipid unsaturation. PMC9183074.p006.s003: Thus, to decrease membrane fluidity under HT, lipid unsaturations could be decreased by downregulating the fatty acid desaturase (FAD) gene. PMC9183074.p006.s004: Maintaining the optimum composition of unsaturated fatty acids is important in determining cellular membrane fluidity under HT.

PMC9183074.p006.s008: For example, roots of heat-tolerant creeping bentgrass exhibited increased lipid saturation under HT. PMC9183074.p006.s009: A heat-tolerant wheat genotype showed higher amounts of saturated lipids and a decrease in the amount of unsaturated lipids. PMC9183074.p006.s010: Similarly, Brassica carinata cv.

events.event_label: HT decreases_abundance_of unsaturated linolenic acid (18:3)
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p006.s009: A heat-tolerant wheat genotype showed higher amounts of saturated lipids and a decrease in the amount of unsaturated lipids. PMC9183074.p006.s010: Similarly, Brassica carinata cv. PMC9183074.p006.s011: Avanza 641 responded to HT by decreasing the level of unsaturation in membrane lipid, especially in unsaturated linolenic acid (18:3) to maintain optimal membrane fluidity. PMC9183074.p006.s012: Moreover, soybean and peanut also responded to HT by decreasing lipid unsaturation levels to maintain membrane fluidity, and the magnitudes of reduction were greater for heat-tolerant than for heat-sensitive genotypes. PMC9183074.p006.s013: In contrast, some experiments demonstrated that higher degrees of unsaturated fatty acids can maintain membrane fluidity under HT.

events.event_label: HT reduces_or_impairs PSII efficiency
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p007.s006: Additionally, at panicle initiation (PI), HT causes spikelet degeneration and damages the development of flower organs. PMC9183074.p007.s007: The reduction in spikelet fertility can lead to a severe reduction in rice yield. PMC9183074.p007.s008: Several reports in rice and other plants showed that HT adversely affected PSII efficiency. PMC9183074.p007.s009: A lower maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm) was found in tall fescue grass exposed to 35/30 °C (day/night) for 20 days, and in spinach stressed at 50 °C. PMC9183074.p007.s010: In rice, significantly reduced Fv/Fm was reported when plants were subjected to heat stress at 40–55 °C for 30 min at seedling, 35 and 40 °C at tillering, 45 °C for 30 min at heading and 42 °C for 30 min at dough grain stage.

events.event_label: HT stress decreases_abundance_of Fv/Fm
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p010.s001: Rice plants of all four cultivars in the control condition had the maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency in the dark-adapted state (Fv/Fm) values equal to or higher than 0.80, indicating the absence of damage to PSII, with CN1 showing the highest value at 0.822 (Figure 1). PMC9183074.p010.s002: However, when subjected to HT stress, Fv/Fm significantly decreased in IR64 (the heat sensitive check), KDML105 and CN1. PMC9183074.p010.s003: In contrast, the heat-tolerant genotype (N22) maintained a high Fv/Fm value (approximately 0.806). PMC9183074.p010.s004: Heat stressed CN1 still had relatively high Fv/Fm that was slightly higher than N22.

PMC9183074.p019.s002: Six-unsaturated fatty acids including linoleic acid (C18:2), γ-Linolenic acid (C18:3n6), linolenic acid (C18:3), cis-5,8,11,14,17-eicosapentaenoic acid (C20:5), oleic acid (C18:1) and erucic acid (C22:1) were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars under both conditions. evidence.text: This study aimed at characterizing the HT response in terms of PSII efficiency and membrane stability, and to identify leaf fatty acid changes that may be associated with HT tolerance or sensitivity of rice genotypes.

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s001: Rice cultivated in the tropics is exposed to high temperature (HT) stress which threatens its growth and survival. PMC9183074.p002.s002: This study aimed at characterizing the HT response in terms of PSII efficiency and membrane stability, and to identify leaf fatty acid changes that may be associated with HT tolerance or sensitivity of rice genotypes. evidence_context_sentences.text: Rice cultivated in the tropics is exposed to high temperature (HT) stress which threatens its growth and survival. This study aimed at characterizing the HT response in terms of PSII efficiency and membrane stability, and to identify leaf fatty acid changes that may be associated with HT tolerance or sensitivity of rice genotypes.
Twenty-eight-day-old seedlings of two Thai rice cultivars (CN1 and KDML105), a standard heat tolerance (N22), and a heat sensitive (IR64) rice genotype were treated at 42 °C for 7 days.

Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm).
Compared to KDML105 and IR64, CN1 was more tolerant of HT, showing a lower increase in EL and MDA, and less reduction in Fv/Fm.

relations.subject: HT

evidence.text: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm).

relations.evidence_preview: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s003: Twenty-eight-day-old seedlings of two Thai rice cultivars (CN1 and KDML105), a standard heat tolerance (N22), and a heat sensitive (IR64) rice genotype were treated at 42 °C for 7 days. PMC9183074.p002.s004: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm). PMC9183074.p002.s005: Compared to KDML105 and IR64, CN1 was more tolerant of HT, showing a lower increase in EL and MDA, and less reduction in Fv/Fm. PMC9183074.p002.s006: N22 and CN1 showed a higher percentage reduction of unsaturated fatty acids (C18:2 and C18:3), which are the major components of the thylakoid membrane, rendering the optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and intactness of PSII complex. PMC9183074.p002.s007: Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm).
Compared to KDML105 and IR64, CN1 was more tolerant of HT, showing a lower increase in EL and MDA, and less reduction in Fv/Fm.

N22 and CN1 showed a higher percentage reduction of unsaturated fatty acids (C18:2 and C18:3), which are the major components of the thylakoid membrane, rendering the optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and intactness of PSII complex.
Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions.
Dramatic increases in long-chain fatty acids may lead to cuticular wax synthesis which provides protective roles for heat tolerance.

relations.subject: HT

evidence.text: Several aspects of HT effects on PSII have been reported, including the disassociation of the PSII peripheral antenna complex from the core complex, inactivation and disassociation of oxygen-evolving PSII complexes, and inhibition of electron transport from QA to QB.

relations.evidence_preview: Several aspects of HT effects on PSII have been reported, including the disassociation of the PSII peripheral antenna complex from the core complex, inactivation and disassociation of oxygen-evolving PSII complexes, and inhibition of electron transport from QA to QB.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p004.s006: HT affects PSII by significantly decreasing its function. PMC9183074.p004.s007: Several aspects of HT effects on PSII have been reported, including the disassociation of the PSII peripheral antenna complex from the core complex, inactivation and disassociation of oxygen-evolving PSII complexes, and inhibition of electron transport from QA to QB. PMC9183074.p004.s008: The photosynthetic carbon fixation is disrupted by temperatures above optimum for plant function. PMC9183074.p004.s009: This commonly coincides with chlorophyll degradation (caused by reactive oxygen species; ROS) resulting in rapid leaf senescence.

evidence_context_sentences.text: HT affects PSII by significantly decreasing its function.

Several aspects of HT effects on PSII have been reported, including the disassociation of the PSII peripheral antenna complex from the core complex, inactivation and disassociation of oxygen-evolving PSII complexes, and inhibition of electron transport from QA to QB.
The photosynthetic carbon fixation is disrupted by temperatures above optimum for plant function.
This commonly coincides with chlorophyll degradation (caused by reactive oxygen species; ROS) resulting in rapid leaf senescence.

evidence.text: Elevated temperature can impose adverse effects on rice at different growth stages (such as germination, seedling, tillering, reproductive and grain filling stages) and induce reductions in rice yield.

relations.evidence_preview: Elevated temperature can impose adverse effects on rice at different growth stages (such as germination, seedling, tillering, reproductive and grain filling stages) and induce reductions in rice yield.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p007.s003: Rice can be cultivated and grows well in a wide geographical and climatic range. PMC9183074.p007.s004: Elevated temperature can impose adverse effects on rice at different growth stages (such as germination, seedling, tillering, reproductive and grain filling stages) and induce reductions in rice yield. PMC9183074.p007.s005: At the flowering stage, HT reduces spikelet fertility by inhibiting anther dehiscence and hence decreasing pollination. PMC9183074.p007.s006: Additionally, at panicle initiation (PI), HT causes spikelet degeneration and damages the development of flower organs. PMC9183074.p007.s007: The reduction in spikelet fertility can lead to a severe reduction in rice yield.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Especially in Asia, rice is necessary for household consumption, food products and the national economy.

Additionally, at panicle initiation (PI), HT causes spikelet degeneration and damages the development of flower organs.

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p010.s001: Rice plants of all four cultivars in the control condition had the maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency in the dark-adapted state (Fv/Fm) values equal to or higher than 0.80, indicating the absence of damage to PSII, with CN1 showing the highest value at 0.822 (Figure 1). PMC9183074.p010.s002: However, when subjected to HT stress, Fv/Fm significantly decreased in IR64 (the heat sensitive check), KDML105 and CN1. evidence_context_sentences.text: Rice plants of all four cultivars in the control condition had the maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency in the dark-adapted state (Fv/Fm) values equal to or higher than 0.
80, indicating the absence of damage to PSII, with CN1 showing the highest value at 0.
822 (Figure 1).
However, when subjected to HT stress, Fv/Fm significantly decreased in IR64 (the heat sensitive check), KDML105 and CN1.

evidence.text: A total of nine saturated fatty acids were identified from rice seedlings, and the content of each acid under the control and HT treatment are displayed in Table 1.

relations.evidence_preview: A total of nine saturated fatty acids were identified from rice seedlings, and the content of each acid under the control and HT treatment are displayed in Table 1.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p017.s001: A total of nine saturated fatty acids were identified from rice seedlings, and the content of each acid under the control and HT treatment are displayed in Table 1. PMC9183074.p017.s002: Eight saturated fatty acids, namely lauric acid (C12:0), myristic acid (C14:0), pentadecanoic acid (C15:0), palmitic acid (C16:0), stearic acid (C18:0), arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0), were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars in both control and HT conditions. PMC9183074.p017.s003: Only one saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), was found only in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p017.s004: Moreover, capric acid contents in HT-stressed CN1 and KDML105 exhibited significant increments.

evidence_context_sentences.text: A total of nine saturated fatty acids were identified from rice seedlings, and the content of each acid under the control and HT treatment are displayed in Table 1.

Eight saturated fatty acids, namely lauric acid (C12:0), myristic acid (C14:0), pentadecanoic acid (C15:0), palmitic acid (C16:0), stearic acid (C18:0), arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0), were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars in both control and HT conditions.
Only one saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), was found only in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105.
Moreover, capric acid contents in HT-stressed CN1 and KDML105 exhibited significant increments.

relations.subject: C18:2

evidence.text: Six-unsaturated fatty acids including linoleic acid (C18:2), γ-Linolenic acid (C18:3n6), linolenic acid (C18:3), cis-5,8,11,14,17-eicosapentaenoic acid (C20:5), oleic acid (C18:1) and erucic acid (C22:1) were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars under both conditions.

relations.evidence_preview: Six-unsaturated fatty acids including linoleic acid (C18:2), γ-Linolenic acid (C18:3n6), linolenic acid (C18:3), cis-5,8,11,14,17-eicosapentaenoic acid (C20:5), oleic acid (C18:1) and erucic acid (C22:1) were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars under both conditions.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p019.s001: A total of nine unsaturated fatty acids were identified, and the contents under the control and HT treatment are shown in Table 2. PMC9183074.p019.s002: Six-unsaturated fatty acids including linoleic acid (C18:2), γ-Linolenic acid (C18:3n6), linolenic acid (C18:3), cis-5,8,11,14,17-eicosapentaenoic acid (C20:5), oleic acid (C18:1) and erucic acid (C22:1) were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars under both conditions. PMC9183074.p019.s003: Cis-11,14-eicosadienoic acid (C20:2) which was present in the non-stress seedlings of all cultivars completely disappeared from all HT-stressed seedlings. PMC9183074.p019.s004: In the control seedlings, this acid was found in slightly greater amounts in the Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105.

evidence_context_sentences.text: A total of nine unsaturated fatty acids were identified, and the contents under the control and HT treatment are shown in Table 2.

Six-unsaturated fatty acids including linoleic acid (C18:2), γ-Linolenic acid (C18:3n6), linolenic acid (C18:3), cis-5,8,11,14,17-eicosapentaenoic acid (C20:5), oleic acid (C18:1) and erucic acid (C22:1) were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars under both conditions.
Cis-11,14-eicosadienoic acid (C20:2) which was present in the non-stress seedlings of all cultivars completely disappeared from all HT-stressed seedlings.
In the control seedlings, this acid was found in slightly greater amounts in the Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105.

relations.subject: HT

evidence.text: It decreases photosynthetic efficiency and yield.

relations.evidence_preview: It decreases photosynthetic efficiency and yield.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p024.s001: High temperature (HT) is harmful to plant growth and survival. PMC9183074.p024.s002: It decreases photosynthetic efficiency and yield. PMC9183074.p024.s003: HT can induce excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, resulting in cellular membrane injury, impaired photosynthetic mechanism and oxygen evolving complex associated with PSII, and inhibition of protein synthesis. PMC9183074.p024.s004: Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction.

evidence_context_sentences.text: High temperature (HT) is harmful to plant growth and survival. It decreases photosynthetic efficiency and yield.

HT can induce excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, resulting in cellular membrane injury, impaired photosynthetic mechanism and oxygen evolving complex associated with PSII, and inhibition of protein synthesis.
Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction.
The chloroplast photosynthetic function is supposed to be the most HT-sensitive cellular function, and PSII is one of the most sensitive to HT.

relations.subject: HT

evidence.text: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT.

relations.evidence_preview: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p025.s001: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT. PMC9183074.p025.s002: The thylakoid membrane, where the photosynthetic light reaction occurs, is also highly thermal sensitive. PMC9183074.p025.s003: HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death. PMC9183074.p025.s004: MDA content and EL are sufficient indicators of membrane thermostability, and they can be considered directly related to the degree of plant damage by HT.

evidence_context_sentences.text: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT.
The thylakoid membrane, where the photosynthetic light reaction occurs, is also highly thermal sensitive.

HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death.
MDA content and EL are sufficient indicators of membrane thermostability, and they can be considered directly related to the degree of plant damage by HT.

evidence_context_sentences.text: HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death.
MDA content and EL are sufficient indicators of membrane thermostability, and they can be considered directly related to the degree of plant damage by HT.

In this study, CN1 and N22 exhibited slightly decreased membrane thermostability, as indicated by a small increase in EL and MDA content (Figure 2), but IR64 and aromatic Thai rice KDML105 displayed a sharp increase in EL and MDA content under HT (Figure 2).

Pansarakham et al. previously reported that MDA and EL of rice cvs. IR64, KDML105, and PTT1 significantly increased after an exposure to 42 °C for 7 days while those of Dular and N22 remained unchanged. This suggested that HT caused a reduction in membrane thermostability in IR64 and KDML105 and resulted in markedly decreased photosynthetic efficiency of PSII (Fv/Fm) (Figure 1).

Thylakoid membrane is the site where light harvesting pigments, PSII, PSI and electron carrier proteins are embedded. Thus, the thylakoid membrane-increased fluidity due to HT resulted in weakness in the bonding between lipid and electron carrier protein molecules.

relations.triple: HT decreases_abundance_of total fatty acid content
relations.subject: HT

evidence.text: Under HT, total fatty acid content decreased in all rice cultivars (Figure 3, Table 1).

relations.evidence_preview: Under HT, total fatty acid content decreased in all rice cultivars (Figure 3, Table 1).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s003: Diverse plant lipids consist of polyunsaturated fatty acids, mainly linoleic acid (C18:2) and linolenic acid (C18:3), monounsaturated fatty acids, mainly oleic acid (C18:1) and saturated fatty acids, mainly palmitic acid (C16:0) and stearic acid (C18:0). PMC9183074.p026.s004: An insight into the relationship between the observed physiological parameters under HT and alterations in leaf fatty acid content and composition was manifested in this study. PMC9183074.p026.s005: Under HT, total fatty acid content decreased in all rice cultivars (Figure 3, Table 1). PMC9183074.p026.s006: A comparison between the two Thai rice cultivars revealed that under HT, CN1 showed the lowest reduction compared to the control and contained the highest total fatty acid content, but KDML105 showed the highest reduction and the lowest amount of total fatty acids (Figure 3, Table 1). PMC9183074.p026.s007: This suggested that HT significantly changed total leaf fatty acids, and different genotypes were affected to different degrees.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Diverse plant lipids consist of polyunsaturated fatty acids, mainly linoleic acid (C18:2) and linolenic acid (C18:3), monounsaturated fatty acids, mainly oleic acid (C18:1) and saturated fatty acids, mainly palmitic acid (C16:0) and stearic acid (C18:0).
An insight into the relationship between the observed physiological parameters under HT and alterations in leaf fatty acid content and composition was manifested in this study.
Under HT, total fatty acid content decreased in all rice cultivars (Figure 3, Table 1).

A comparison between the two Thai rice cultivars revealed that under HT, CN1 showed the lowest reduction compared to the control and contained the highest total fatty acid content, but KDML105 showed the highest reduction and the lowest amount of total fatty acids (Figure 3, Table 1).
This suggested that HT significantly changed total leaf fatty acids, and different genotypes were affected to different degrees.

evidence_context_sentences.text: A comparison between the two Thai rice cultivars revealed that under HT, CN1 showed the lowest reduction compared to the control and contained the highest total fatty acid content, but KDML105 showed the highest reduction and the lowest amount of total fatty acids (Figure 3, Table 1).

The thylakoid membrane is reported to be the most sensitive site severely damaged by heat stress and the degree of damage is related to plant survival. The major types of lipids in the thylakoid membrane are galactolipids (monogalactosyldiacylglycerol, MGDG and digalactosyldiacylglycerol, DGDG), sulfolipids (sulfoquinovosyldiacylglycerol, SQDG), and phospholipids (phosphatidylglycerol, PG).
Of all lipids in thylakoids, MGDG and DGDG can reach 52% and 26%, respectively.
The ratio of MGDG to DGDG can indicate the photosynthetic membrane stability.

evidence_context_sentences.text: A comparison between the two Thai rice cultivars revealed that under HT, CN1 showed the lowest reduction compared to the control and contained the highest total fatty acid content, but KDML105 showed the highest reduction and the lowest amount of total fatty acids (Figure 3, Table 1).
This suggested that HT significantly changed total leaf fatty acids, and different genotypes were affected to different degrees.

The thylakoid membrane is reported to be the most sensitive site severely damaged by heat stress and the degree of damage is related to plant survival. The major types of lipids in the thylakoid membrane are galactolipids (monogalactosyldiacylglycerol, MGDG and digalactosyldiacylglycerol, DGDG), sulfolipids (sulfoquinovosyldiacylglycerol, SQDG), and phospholipids (phosphatidylglycerol, PG).
Of all lipids in thylakoids, MGDG and DGDG can reach 52% and 26%, respectively.
The ratio of MGDG to DGDG can indicate the photosynthetic membrane stability.

relations.triple: Unsaturated fatty acids is_detected_in thylakoid membrane
relations.subject: Unsaturated fatty acids

evidence.text: Unsaturated fatty acids constitute 75% of total fatty acids in the thylakoid membrane, while the saturated fatty acid composition was approximately 25%.

relations.evidence_preview: Unsaturated fatty acids constitute 75% of total fatty acids in the thylakoid membrane, while the saturated fatty acid composition was approximately 25%.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s012: The major types of fatty acids that constitute lipids (MGDG, DGDG, SQDG and PG) in the plant thylakoid membrane are palmitic acid (C16:0), palmitoleic acid (C16:1), roughanic acid (C16:3), oleic acid (C18:1), linoleic acid (C18:2), and linolenic acid (C18:3). PMC9183074.p026.s013: Unsaturated fatty acids constitute 75% of total fatty acids in the thylakoid membrane, while the saturated fatty acid composition was approximately 25%. PMC9183074.p026.s014: The total saturated fatty acid content under HT remained unchanged in CN1 but significantly declined in KDML105 (Figure 4, Table 1). PMC9183074.p026.s015: CN1 also contained the highest, and KDML105 the lowest amount of saturated fatty acids under HT. PMC9183074.p026.s016: This suggested that, in CN1, lipid saturation level was well maintained under HT when associated with lower lipid peroxidation, a smaller increase in membrane fluidity, and lower EL (Figure 2).

evidence_context_sentences.text: Unsaturated fatty acids constitute 75% of total fatty acids in the thylakoid membrane, while the saturated fatty acid composition was approximately 25%.
The total saturated fatty acid content under HT remained unchanged in CN1 but significantly declined in KDML105 (Figure 4, Table 1).
CN1 also contained the highest, and KDML105 the lowest amount of saturated fatty acids under HT.
This suggested that, in CN1, lipid saturation level was well maintained under HT when associated with lower lipid peroxidation, a smaller increase in membrane fluidity, and lower EL (Figure 2).
A higher lipid saturation level is also associated with greater membrane stability and higher efficiency of PSII photochemistry (Figure 1).

evidence.text: In contrast to this study, Wang et al. reported that for temperate species such as Arabidopsis, heat shock at 38 °C for 6 h did not induce any changes in unsaturated fatty acids but prolonged treatment at warm temperature (28 °C, 7 d), resulting in an enrichment of these acids to achieve an optimum level of membrane fluidity.

relations.subject: heat stress

evidence.text: Most previous studies focused on the changes in lipid composition of isolated thylakoid membranes under heat stress.

relations.evidence_preview: Most previous studies focused on the changes in lipid composition of isolated thylakoid membranes under heat stress.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p027.s001: Most previous studies focused on the changes in lipid composition of isolated thylakoid membranes under heat stress. PMC9183074.p027.s002: Our study analysed fatty acid compositions of the whole leaf; therefore, many fatty acids not previously reported in heat-stress studies were identified and displayed some interesting patterns of responses. PMC9183074.p027.s003: Among saturated fatty acids, arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0) (Table 1) sharply increased in N22, CN1 and IR64, while these acids significantly reduced in KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s004: In addition, erucic acid (C22:3), a long-chain unsaturated fatty acid, increased in response to HT in N22 and CN1, but significantly reduced in KDML105 and IR64 (Table 2).

evidence_context_sentences.text: Most previous studies focused on the changes in lipid composition of isolated thylakoid membranes under heat stress.

Our study analysed fatty acid compositions of the whole leaf; therefore, many fatty acids not previously reported in heat-stress studies were identified and displayed some interesting patterns of responses.

Among saturated fatty acids, arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0) (Table 1) sharply increased in N22, CN1 and IR64, while these acids significantly reduced in KDML105.
In addition, erucic acid (C22:3), a long-chain unsaturated fatty acid, increased in response to HT in N22 and CN1, but significantly reduced in KDML105 and IR64 (Table 2).

relations.subject: HT

evidence.text: In addition, erucic acid (C22:3), a long-chain unsaturated fatty acid, increased in response to HT in N22 and CN1, but significantly reduced in KDML105 and IR64 (Table 2).
